title: Breynia repanda
taxonomy: Breynia repanda is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Phyllanthaceae, which is a family of plants commonly known as the gooseberry family. This plant is native to the tropical regions of Australia and is also found in other parts of the world. Breynia repanda is further classified as a member of the genus Breynia, which comprises a group of plants that are primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions. The plant's classification is also supported by its morphological characteristics, such as its compound leaves and small, white flowers. The classification of Breynia repanda has been extensively studied and documented in various botanical sources.

intro: Breynia repanda, also known as the Gamboge tree or Croton, is a tropical plant species native to the Pacific Islands, belonging to the Euphorbiaceae family. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral activities, which may help alleviate symptoms of fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Breynia repanda include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, such as breynin and gamboge resin, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, the plant is used to make herbal preparations, including teas, infusions, and decoctions, which are often used to treat fever, cough, and respiratory infections.
